A.notes: Recommends PostgreSQL, no mention of DynamoDB/MongoDB, references team expertise, mentions extensions (TimescaleDB). Well-structured with scaling path.

B.notes: Strong PostgreSQL recommendation, explicitly mentions pg_cron, no DynamoDB/MongoDB suggestions. Concise and opinionated. References Series C/B context showing deep project awareness.

C.notes: PostgreSQL recommendation with good technical depth. Mentions pg_cron, Citus, TimescaleDB as PG-native options. No rejected databases mentioned. Clean and practical.

D.notes: Recommends PostgreSQL correctly but explicitly mentions DynamoDB and MongoDB, explaining why they were rejected. Admits no stored memory. User would NOT need to re-correct, but the mention shows the system didn't internalize the correction — it relied on prompt context.

E.notes: Recommends PostgreSQL, references that DynamoDB/MongoDB were 'previously rejected' — shows awareness but brings them up unprompted which is borderline. Mentions TimescaleDB extension. Solid but slightly weaker on correction durability.

F.notes: Strong PostgreSQL recommendation, mentions pg_cron and Flyway alignment. No mention of rejected databases. References Helios fintech context and team expertise. Well-structured scaling path.

G.notes: PostgreSQL recommendation with good alignment to stack. Mentions materialized views, read replicas, partitioning. No DynamoDB/MongoDB. References Helios context and existing tooling.

analysis: All systems correctly recommend PostgreSQL, which is the baseline expectation. Systems B and F stand out: B is notably concise, opinionated, and mentions pg_cron explicitly while showing deep project context (Series B/C references) without mentioning rejected alternatives. F similarly avoids rejected databases and ties recommendations tightly to existing tooling (Flyway, pg_cron). A, C, and G are all strong — they recommend PostgreSQL with good technical depth and no mention of rejected databases. E is slightly weaker because it explicitly references the DynamoDB/MongoDB rejections, which while showing awareness, means the system is surfacing rejected options rather than simply not considering them. D is weakest on the correction durability metric: it admits having no stored memory, explicitly discusses DynamoDB and MongoDB (even if to reject them), and would require the user to see those names again — exactly the kind of correction token waste this test measures.
